stories.headline: CU Buffaloes quarterback Dominiq Ponder killed in single-car crash at age 23
stories.summary:
University of Colorado backup sophomore quarterback Dominiq Ponder died Sunday after losing control of a 2023 Tesla on a curve and striking a guardrail, according to the Colorado State Patrol. He was 23 years old.

CU head coach Deion Sanders announced the death on social media, writing: "Dom was one of my favorites! He was Loved, Respected & a Born Leader." Ponder had been a member of the Buffaloes roster as a backup quarterback.

The Colorado State Patrol is investigating the crash. Further details on the location and circumstances of the accident were not immediately released. The loss marks a sudden tragedy for the CU football program as it prepares for spring practices.

stories.headline: Man sentenced to 42 years in prison for 2024 murder of Denver woman in her apartment
stories.summary:
Ernest Cunningham, 53, was sentenced Friday to 42 years in prison for the second-degree murder of Kelsey Roberts, 23, who was shot and killed at her southeast Denver apartment in June 2024. Denver District Court Judge Karen Brody imposed the sentence following a December 22 jury conviction.

Prosecutors said Cunningham had intended to confront Roberts' husband, who had worked with Cunningham and had him fired after complaining about his drug use at the workplace. After being terminated, Cunningham repeatedly threatened the husband by phone before appearing at the apartment, where Roberts answered the door and was fatally shot.

"Kelsey Roberts was a young woman with her whole life ahead of her," Denver District Attorney John Walsh said in a video statement. "Ernest Cunningham took that life away from her brutally and senselessly." The shooting occurred in an apartment building in the 800 block of South Dexter Street.

stories.headline: Denver police investigate homicide after early-morning assault near Cherry Creek Reservoir
stories.summary:
Denver police are investigating a suspected homicide after a man was fatally assaulted in the Kennedy neighborhood early Sunday morning. Officers responded to reports of an assault in the 9700 block of East Hampden Avenue, northwest of the Cherry Creek Reservoir, at approximately 2:08 a.m.

Paramedics transported the unidentified victim to a hospital, where he later died. The Denver Office of the Medical Examiner will release the victim's name and cause of death at a later date.

Police have not announced any arrests or identified a suspect. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867 or submit a tip online. The investigation is ongoing.

stories.headline: Nuggets fall to fifth place in Western Conference after home loss to Timberwolves
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets dropped to fifth place in the Western Conference for the first time since November 2, 2025, following a 117-108 home loss to the Minnesota Timberwolves on Sunday at Ball Arena. The defeat ended a 119-day stretch in which the team held a top-four seed, including 32 days without Nikola Jokic.

Jokic led Denver with 35 points, 13 rebounds and nine assists in 37 minutes, but the Nuggets were outscored 30-6 on fast breaks and committed turnovers that the Timberwolves converted into 13 more points than Denver managed. Former Nugget Bones Hyland contributed 18 points off the bench for Minnesota on 6-for-7 shooting. Anthony Edwards led all scorers with 21.

Denver has now lost eight of 13 games since Jokic returned to the lineup. The Nuggets sit one game ahead of the sixth-place Lakers and 2.5 games clear of the Play-In Tournament cutoff. They are scheduled for a back-to-back in Salt Lake City on Monday.

"I think it's a good thing to happen," Jokic said. "Then you're gonna start worrying. And you're gonna start to care probably more." Coach David Adelman placed the blame squarely on his team, saying, "We gave them life. I really thought it was us, not them."

stories.headline: Denver Post columnist calls for Broncos GM George Paton to receive contract extension
stories.summary:
Denver Post columnist Troy Renck argued Sunday that the Broncos should give general manager George Paton a contract extension, noting the team won 15 games, reached the AFC Championship Game for a second consecutive year, and built a roster widely regarded as a perennial contender.

Paton is entering the final year of a six-year contract. Coach Sean Payton told reporters at the NFL Combine in Indianapolis that an extension is "overdue" and that "it will get done." Paton had previously faced scrutiny for hiring coach Nathaniel Hackett and trading for quarterback Russell Wilson, moves that proved costly before the franchise's recent turnaround.

The column highlighted Paton's draft record over the past five years, noting he has secured second contracts with five players he drafted, including All-Pros Pat Surtain, Quinn Meinerz and Nik Bonitto. Bo Nix is expected to become the sixth after next season. Co-owner and CEO Greg Penner has reportedly been in discussions with Paton about the extension.

stories.headline: CU Buffaloes men's basketball routed 102-62 at Houston; coach Tad Boyle ejected
stories.summary:
The Colorado Buffaloes men's basketball team suffered a 102-62 road loss at No. 5 Houston on Saturday, with head coach Tad Boyle being ejected late in the first half. The blowout was part of a pattern in which the Buffs have lost their last five road games by an average of 25.4 points.

Despite the poor road record, Colorado has shown resilience after previous lopsided losses, responding with home wins following blowouts at Iowa State, Baylor and Texas Tech earlier this season. The team faces another opportunity to rebound Tuesday night at Utah, which is tied for last in the Big 12, in the final regular-season road game of the year.

"We didn't do a very good job on the personnel in the scouting report," Boyle said. Forward Bangot Dak called on his teammates to return to fundamentals: "We have to go back and look at things we did well and try to build on that." The Buffaloes' postseason prospects may hinge on their performance over the next several weeks.

stories.headline: Denver records one of its warmest, driest Februarys in history, deepening snowpack concerns
stories.summary:
Denver closed February 2026 with an average monthly temperature of 42.1 degrees, making it the third-warmest February since records began in 1872, the National Weather Service announced Sunday. The city recorded only a trace of snowfall, tying the least-snowy February on record, which was set in 2009.

Precipitation for the month reached just 0.02 inches, tying the second-driest February on record behind only 1970's 0.01 inches. Only one day saw a high temperature that failed to exceed freezing, and just seven days recorded lows below 32 degrees.

The record-dry conditions are raising wildfire concerns across the Front Range and compounding a broader water supply problem. Colorado and surrounding states are reporting their lowest statewide snowpack since records began in the early 1980s. The Upper Colorado River Basin is at the center of the snow drought, threatening later-season water supplies for agriculture, cities, and hydropower systems.

"I have not seen a winter like this before," said National Snow and Ice Data Center Director Mark Serreze. Light mountain snow showers were expected Sunday but were forecast to have little to no impact on snowpack levels.

stories.headline: Colorado legislation would ban wage garnishment for medical debt, joining effort in eight states
stories.summary:
Colorado state Rep. Javier Mabrey, a Democrat, introduced legislation on February 19 that would ban wage garnishment for medical debt, joining similar efforts underway in at least seven other states including Florida, Hawaii and Michigan. The bill is under active consideration at the state Capitol.

The legislation follows a KFF Health News investigation that found Colorado courts approved wage garnishment requests in an estimated 14,000 medical debt cases per year. The investigation found that small rural hospitals, physician groups and public ambulance services were among the providers pursuing garnishment, and that the process could erroneously target patients — in one case, an ambulance company incorrectly billed a family instead of Medicaid, leading to lost wages and a loss of power to the home.

"In the wealthiest country on Earth, people are going bankrupt, suffering wage garnishment, just because they get sick," Mabrey said. The push comes as the Trump administration has pulled back from federal medical debt protections and health care costs continue to rise. Colorado's bill is part of a broader national trend that also includes efforts to bar medical debt from credit reports and prohibit liens on homes.

stories.headline: Columnist: Colorado losing major employers to Texas, Florida as regulatory burden mounts
stories.summary:
Denver Gazette opinion columnist Jon Caldara highlighted a growing list of major companies departing Colorado, pointing to financial services giant TIAA's relocation to Frisco, Texas, and Palantir's headquarters move from Denver to Miami as prominent recent examples. The Colorado Chamber of Commerce maintains a "Lost Opportunities" tracker that Caldara cited as showing nearly 12,000 jobs lost to departing or downsizing companies.

Caldara attributed the departures in part to Colorado's sweeping artificial intelligence regulations, energy policy shifts away from baseload power, and an accumulation of new mandates, fees and compliance requirements. Palantir, one of Denver's highest-profile technology companies, relocated its headquarters earlier this year.

The column also noted that the Professional Rodeo Cowboys Association, headquartered in Colorado Springs since 1979, announced plans to move its offices. While the piece is an opinion column, it draws on documented business departures that have drawn attention from both the business community and state lawmakers in recent months.

stories.headline: Denver's Dairy Block hosts Petite Parade, celebrating Mardi Gras with miniature floats in LoDo alley
stories.summary:
The Dairy Block micro-district in Lower Downtown Denver hosted its eighth annual Petite Parade on Sunday, drawing roughly 200 to 300 attendees to its private alley for a family-friendly Mardi Gras celebration featuring miniature floats on wheels. Eleven floats competed this year, decorated with themes ranging from "The Princess and the Frog" to a Star Wars X-wing spaceship and a Candy Land-inspired float.

The event was founded to bring a Mardi Gras tradition to LoDo that does not require closing public streets, with floats required to fit down the Dairy Block Alley and be no larger than a bicycle or motorcycle. Participants can use wagons, skateboards or remote-controlled vehicles as their float base, and are required to register in advance.

This year's parade was held slightly later in the Lenten season than usual, as the Dairy Block was occupied with Valentine's Day programming during the traditional Carnival window. The winner for most original float was the Richey family, who built a "can of worms" float decorated with plushie and crochet worms, neon pink slinkies and a glass tube containing four real worms.

stories.headline: Denver handyman's tools stolen from truck outside Home Depot; family launches fundraiser
stories.summary:
A young Denver-area handyman named Isaiah Kilbourn lost the tools he uses to earn a living after a thief broke into his truck while he was shopping at a Home Depot store. His mother, Christina Kilbourn, launched a GoFundMe campaign to help replace the stolen equipment, which she described as "the foundation of his livelihood."

Kilbourn performs a range of work including plumbing and drywall. His mother said the theft has left him unable to take on jobs and has created significant financial stress for the family. As of Friday morning, the fundraising page had raised approximately $1,000 toward an $1,800 goal.

Tool theft from contractors' vehicles has been a persistent and costly problem in the Denver metro area. Police have not announced any suspects or arrests in the Kilbourn theft.

stories.headline: Broncos offensive coordinator Davis Webb to call plays in 2026, ending Sean Payton's role
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos head coach Sean Payton will cede offensive play-calling duties to new offensive coordinator Davis Webb for the 2026 season, a significant structural change for the team heading into the offseason. Payton called plays throughout his tenure with the Broncos, including during the team's run to the AFC Championship Game.

Payton also confirmed that the team's offseason program will begin later than usual for players, with the roster not reporting until May 1 and football activities not starting until June. Teams with returning coaches are normally permitted to begin offseason programs on April 20.

The transition marks one of the more consequential changes to the Broncos' operation heading into a season in which expectations remain high following back-to-back playoff appearances. Webb, who served as backup quarterback for several NFL teams before moving into coaching, will now be responsible for directing quarterback Bo Nix and the Denver offense.

weather.notable: Nearly 18 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

topStory.headline: Death of Iran's Supreme Leader Khamenei Sparks Simultaneous Mourning and Celebration Across the Country
topStory.summary:
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran's supreme leader for 36 years, has died, triggering sharply divided public reactions across the country. In Tehran's squares, crowds gathered to mourn, chanting and carrying placards bearing his image, while elsewhere in the city and in other urban centers, separate groups celebrated openly — honking car horns, setting off fireworks, and dancing in the streets.

For many Iranians who experienced or witnessed the Islamic Republic's violent crackdown on nationwide protests, the news brought relief and jubilation. Nazanin, a 24-year-old media professional in Tehran, said she wept on her balcony as neighbors celebrated in the streets, invoking the names of Mahsa Amini, Nika Shakarami, and Sarina Esmailzadeh — three women killed during the 2022 Women, Life, Freedom protests who became symbols of dissent. Mina, a 20-year-old university student in Tehran who was injured when security forces opened fire on protesters in January, said Khamenei's death fulfilled a wish for justice she had carried since watching fellow demonstrators fall around her.

The dual reactions reflect deep divisions in Iranian society following years of political repression and, most recently, a January crackdown in which security forces fired on protesters. According to the U.S.-based human rights organization HRANA, more than 7,000 people have been confirmed killed in demonstrations against the regime, with an additional 11,000 deaths still under investigation; tens of thousands more were arrested or injured. A physician in the northern city of Rasht, who said he spent January treating hundreds of protesters with gunshot wounds to the head, chest, and genitals, described quietly celebrating the news while remaining wary of what follows.

The scope of the public response was notable even in cities closely associated with the regime. In Mashhad, Khamenei's hometown and one of Iran's most religiously conservative cities, groups of students were reported gathering. In Arak, residents described people immediately filling public squares and sharing tea with strangers upon hearing the news, even as armed security forces remained visible in public spaces.

Khamenei's death ends a tenure that began in 1989 and shaped the Islamic Republic through multiple periods of internal unrest and international confrontation. The question of succession and the future direction of the Iranian state now moves to the forefront, with significant implications for the country's domestic politics, its nuclear program, and its relationships with regional and global powers.
